Theory of polyampholyte solutions  Paul G. Higgs; Jean‐François Joanny

Theory of polyampholyte solutions Paul G. Higgs anci Jean-Frangois Joanny G.N.R.S. Institut Charles Sadron, 6 rue Boussingault, 67083 Strasbourg, France

(Received 6 September 1990; accepted 12 October 1990) We consider polyampholytepolymers containing both positive and negative monomers randomly dispersed along the chain. Neutral chains collapse into a globule due to attractive electrostatic interactions. The behavior of the charges inside the globule is similar to that of charges in a small volume of simple electrolyte. A screening length Kp- 1 coming from the polymeric charge may be defined as in Debye-Hiickel theory. The internal structure of the globule is that of close packed blobs of radius equal to the screening length. When salt is added this further screens the interactions and reduces the attractions. The globule begins to increase in size when the concentration of salt becomes larger than the concentration of charge on the polymer itself. Screened Coulomb interactions in a neutral chain behave like a negative contribution to excluded volume. For a chain in a good solvent there is a e salt concentration at which the net excluded volume becomes zero. Chains are swollen above this concentration of salt, and collapsed below this concentration. For small sections of chain the Coulomb interactions are unscreened and cannot be treated as a modification to excluded volume. Chains with a strong net charge of one sign tend to behave as conventional polyelectrolyte with charges of only one sign. We determine the criterion for the value of the net charge at which the repulsions (polyelectrolyte effect) begin to dominate the attractions (polyampholyte effect). The predictions are found to be in good qualitative agreement with experiments.

which we find below were not found by these authors because they do not introduce the positive third virial coeffi~ cient between monomers which turns out to be the term in the free energy that stabilizes the collapsed state. Qian and Kholodenk0 3 have investigated a randomly charged chain in a e solvent, using the replica method to describe the role of the quenched random distribution of charges. Our mechanism for the chain collapse also appears different from the one they propose. Polyampholytes are interesting from a theoretical point of view as examples of chains which undergo a coil-globule transition. 4 For usual uncharged polymers this occurs in a poor solvent where the excludedyolume (second virhd coefficient) is negative. In a more concentrated solution the collapse transition is associated with a segregation between polymer and solvent. For a neutral polyampholyte the attractive interactions can sometimes be treated as a negative contribution to the excluded volume, however the problem is complicated by the long-range nature of the unscreened Coulomb interaction, which behaves qualitatively differently from short-range excluded volume interactions. The problem is also of interest because of its relation to several recent models for the folding of proteins. 5-7 The proteins are modeled as chains with random pair interactions of the excluded volume type. This gives rise to frozen configurations in the collapsed state, reminiscent of the folding of a protein. In certain cases the configuration of a polyampho-' Iyte could also be frozen because of the strong attraction between charges of opposite signs. We will only consider here the other limit where the polyampholyte is at thermodynamie equilibrium. In this paper we give a scaling theory for polyampholytes which contain both positively and negatively charged

chain collapse, but the most important regimes of behavior

monomers, and maybe uncharged monomers. In Sec. II we

0021-9606/91/021543-12$03.00

@ 1991 American Institute of Physics

By "polyampholyte" we mean a polymer containing positively and negatively charged monomers interspersed within' the same linear chain. They may be either neutral (same number of negative as positive monomers) or have a net charge of one sign. If the net charge is hirge we may expect the chain to behave like a conventional polyelectrolyte with all charges of the same sign. The electrostatic forces between monomers are repulsive, and in 'a dilute solution the polymer adopts an extended or rod-like configuration. The counterions are also important, since they insure the overall neutrality of the solution. A large body of experimental and theoretical work exists on the structure of polyelectrolyte solutions (see references below). The behavior of neutral polyampholyte chains is entirely' different:-'the net electrostatic forces are attractive so that the chains have a tendency to collapse into a globule. The origin of this attraction is the screening effect which occurs in ionic solutions. Each charge is surrounded by an "atmosphere" of charges of the opposite sign which screens the long range Coulomb interactions and gives rise to a net gain in electrostatic energy. The counterions are usually llnimportant in this case, and solutioIls can sometimes be prepared with no counterions since the chains are already neutral. " Experimentally, the conformation of polyampholyte chains seems to be controlled by two main parameters, the net electrical charge and the ionic strength. 1 The chains are often insoluble in water due to the strong electrostatic attractions between the monomers. The polymers can be solubilized by the addition of salt, which screens the interactions and weakens the attractions. ' There exist very few theoretical studies' of polyampholytes. Edwards et al. 2 have given a scaling theory for the

I. INTRODUCTION

[Page 3]

discuss neutral polyampholytes starting from physical arguments for the strength oftht:; attractive and repulsive interactions between monomers. The effect of ionic strength is considered in Sec. III along the same lines. These results are rederived in Sec. IV using a functional integral description of the polymer configurations. In Sec. V we consider nonneutral chains; and estimate the value of the net charge at which the electrostatic repulsions (polyelectrolyte effect) dominate the attractions (polyampholyte effect). Section VI is devoted to the phase separation which accompanies the collapse ofthe chains in solution. The last section presents some concluding remarks and a comparison of our results with recent experiments. II. SCALING THEORY FOR NEUTRAL POLYAMPHOl YTE CHAINS

J. Chem. Phys., Vol. 94, No.2, 15 January 1991

We consider a chain of N monomers of size b having a fraction/of positively charged monomers and a fraction g of negatively charged monomers. The charged monomers are assumed to be distributed randomly along the chain. We first study a neutral chain if = g). We suppose that there exists a collapsed state of the chain with a volume significantly smaller than the Gaussian chain volume N 3/ 2 b 3 but significantly larger than the volume oftne completely close-packed monomers Nb 3. The chain in this state is at thermodynamic eqUilibrium and can easily change from one configuration to another. The distribution of the charged monomers within the globule resembles that of the charges in a simple ionic solution rather than those in an ionic crystal. The electrostatic interactions create correlations between the positions in space of the positive and negative charges so that each charge tends to be surrounded by a shell of charge of the opposite sign. The Coulomb interactions are thus screened at long distances by the presence of other intervening charges forming this polarization shell. We conjecture that this effect can be treated in the same way for the charges on the polymer chain as for free charges in an ionic solution and calculate the electrostatic energy of the polyampholyte as if it were a small volume of a Debye-Hiickel electrolyte solution. This cannot be justified rigorously, however we will proceed with the assumption in this section, noting that the same results are obtained in Sec. IV starting from an entirely different point of view. In the Debye-Hiickel theory8 for a solution containing a concentration n of point-like charges e the net gain of electrostatic energy per charge due to the screening effect is

[Page 4]

the Debye-Hiickel theory must be modified to take into account the finite volume of the polymer globule. Repeating the Debye-Hiickel argument for a charge in the center of a sphere, we find that if KpR < 1 the energy (2.1) needs to be reduced by an extra factor of KpR. The modified electrostatic energy again becomes of order kTat N = N l • The finite volume effect therefore does not affect the prediction of the blob size. The polyampholyte theory of Edwards et aU follows the same lines as the one presented here. However, it is argued that the electrostatic attractions are balanced not by the repulsive forces but by the reduction in entropy of the chain due to confinement within a sphere smaller than its natural radius. The third order virial term that we have considered turns out to be always larger than the confinement entropy for a collapsed chain and must be taken into account. Also Eq. (1.1) of Ref. 2 seems to state that short chains collapse and long ones are open, which is contrary to what we find here.

III. NEUTRAL POLYAMPHOlYTE CHAIN IN A SALT SOLUTION

In the presence of salt there is additional screening of the electrostatic interactions and the attractive forces are smaller than in pure water. The addition of salt tends to reswell the collapsed chain back to the preferred dimensions of the uncharged chain. We study one isolated polyampholyte in a solution of concentration no of 1-1 electrolyte, Since the electrostatic energy gained per charge increases with concentration there is a tendency for the free charges to be attracted into the region occupied by the polymer where the fixed polymeric charges are already present. The unscreened Coulomb interaction is long range and cannot be replaced by the pseudopotential. The equivalent "pressure" term in Sec. II A was proportional to p312 and not p2: The behavior of the electrostatic interactions between a pair of monomers depends on the separation of the monomers. Two monomers widely separated along the chain (and therefore widely separated in space on average) interact via ascreened potential, and behave as though there were an effective excluded volume between then. Two monomers close together along the chain interact with the unscreened Coulomb potential. For a full description of the conformation of the chain it is not sufficient just to know the effective excluded volume applicable at large separations. This point is seen clearly in the alternative argument below.

This corresponds to a compact chain of Gaussian blobs of sizeN3 :

We know that the salt tends to increase the polymer radius, therefore the argument is valid only if N3 is larger than the blob size in the absence of saIt Nt. This is true provided Ko > Kmin' where

K min _Jl/b •

This value of Kmin is precisely equal to that of Kp in the collapsed globule in absence of salt. Thus if the salt concentration is larger than the polymeric charge concentration in the collapsed chain the blob size is governed by the salt concentration, and has the value N 3 • If the salt concentration is lower than the polymeric charge concentration the blob size is governed by the polymeric charge, and has the value Nt.

IV. PERTURBATION THEORY AND UNIFORM EXPANSION METHOD

In the previous sections, we have conjectured that the electrostatic interactions in a collapsed polyampholyte chain can be described by the theory of simple electrolytes. We now study the polyampholyte conformation using the socalled uniform expansion method and show that it leads to the same conclusions if we preaverage the charge distribution along the chain. We then summarize our results for the conformation of neutral polyampholytes. Consider a polyampholyte chain in which monomer n has charge ec n , where Cn

The internal pressure includes the term due to the polymeric repulsions PpOI1 the ideal gas term for the salt ions, and the electrostatic correction, which depends on n tot not n I:

Note that the electrostatic potential is zero both inside and outside the globule. This is because both positive and negative salt ions can pass freely between the two regions. There is thus no Donnan effect, and no "membrane potential" to add to the chemical potential p,. For a chain in a good solvent the application of Eq. (3.10) presents a problem. The electrostatic attractions are predicted to vary as p2, the same as the second virial term. On the other hand, it was argued in Sec. II B that P pol _ p9/4 in a good solvent due to the excluded volume correlations. It would be inconsistent to balance the p9/4 term with the p2 electrostatic term. Instead we must argue that the electrostatic attractions represent a negative modification to the excluded volume. We expect the chain to collapse if the modified excluded volume is negative. There is therefore a () compensation point at the salt concentration at which the negative electrostatic contribution exactly balances the positive excluded volume interaction V. This is not surprising since we know that the screened Coulomb interaction is short range, and that short range potentials can be replaced by an excluded volume pseudopotential veffo(r - r '.). where rn is the position of monomer n. We will not as yet be too specific exactly which inverse screening length K we mean in this formula and we will not a priori assume the existence of a value Kp due to the polymeric charge; we suppose that in principle we are entitled to set K = 0 if thereis no salt present. The evaluation of physical properties from the probability distribution is complicated by the quenched random disorder of the cn 'so In order to treat this problem, one is lead to consider the replica method, which copes successfully with performing the quenched average for many problems. For the case v = 0 a replica calculation for the randomly charged chain has been attempted by Qian and Kholodenko. 3 The problem is however extremely difficult, and the results are not entirely conclusive. We avoid these problems here by preaveraging over the charge distribution Cn' We justify this on two grounds. First, we are interested in very general properties of the polyampholyte behavior, such as under what conditions does the chain collapse, and what is the mean density of the collapsed state. We do not expect these properties to be influenced by the particular sequence of charges on the chain. Second, as stated above, we are considering the case where the density iIi. the collapsed state is still fairly low, so that the polymer can still easily change from one configuration in space to another. We thus assume that the polymer is at thermal equilibrium and that the binary interactiQns are .of .order kT. The cQllapsed state is by nQ means frozen into .one single "valley" of configuratiQns in phase space. Such frozen states nQ dQubtexist at very low temperature, or equivalently, fQr very highly charged chains, but we do nQt cQnsider them here. We calculate the mean end-tQ-end distance (R 2) .of the chain using a methQd due to Edwards and Singh 12 which is discussed by Doi and Edwards 11 under the name "uniform expansiQn mQdel." A very similar methQd has been used by Muthukumar 13 fQr polyelectrolytes. If the interactiQns are treated as a perturbation then the perturbatiQn parameter always depends .on Nand therefQre the perturbation expansion has a very limited range .of application. This problem is circumvented by introducing an effective mQnQmer size b i and writing the prQpagatQr as

The uniform expansion method consists of choosing b l so that 6.R 2 is zero. This does not mean that the chain is always Gaussian, with R 2 - N. The choice of b l may be a function of N. in which case R '1. ~ N 2 v for some other vi: 1/2. , Equation (4.6) is rather similar to Eq. (3.18) of Qian and Kholodenko. 3 It contains a two-body term which is zero for neutral chains (their p = 1/2) and an effective attractive term which has come about from various manipulations of the replica Hamiltonian. However their attractive term is proportional to a coefficient 8 which is also zero for perfectly neutral chains. Our interpretation of Ref. 3 is that there should be no collapse for an exactly neutral chain. This seems untrue experimentally. Our theory above has a nonzero attractive term for a neutral chain, which becomes most important at this point when the two-body repulsive term is zero. Shakhnovich and GutinS have considered a chain with a random excluded volume B (n,n') between links n and n'. They set (B(n,n'» = Bo and «B(n,n ' ) - Bo)2) = B 2, and find that the chain collapses into a globule when B - (B 2/2b 3) is negative. In other words, there would be a collapse due to the B 2 term even when Bo = 0. t h'IS .IS very similar to the effect observed in our theory for neutral polyampholytes. The transition in polyampholytes with large excluded volume is not a e transition as for the small excluded volume case. For Ko > Kp we must have a swollen chain, and for Ko < K 0 we must have a collapsed chain, almost as if there were no salt. The present theory is not sufficient to say what happens in the intermediate regime Ke <Ko<Kp. On the one hand, the excluded volume is positive, suggesting that long chains are swollen, while on the other hand the salt concentration is much less than the polymeric charge concentration, suggesting that the polymer should collapse as in absence of salt. Further work is therefore needed to clarify this transition region. It is probable that Eq. (4.11) for the effective excluded volume is only valid once Ko > K p ' and that there is a transition from collapsed to swollen chains at this point without passing through the usual e point.

The / + g of course emerges directly from the argument of Sec. IV by putting Za ~ 1. There is some ambiguity in the physical interpretation: does the screening in the polyampholyte come from all the charges / + g or just the neutral part 2g? The answer would appear to be/ + g from the method of Sec. IV. In any case we are interested in/ - g <f + g, where there is essentially no difference. The overall conformation of the chain is determined by the relative value of Nt and N.. If N, < Ne the attractive polyampholyte interaction dominates. The polymer is collapsed in the same way as a neutral polyampholyte as discussed in the previous section. If N, > Ne the repulsive polyelectrolyte interaction dominates and the chain behaves more like a usual polyelectrolyte. The necessary net charge to swell and thus redissolve the polyampholyte is therefore

[Page 10]

For the small net charge case-reverse of Eq. (5.5)the polyampholyte term dominates at small distances. The net excluded volume v* is always negative in this case and the chain behaves rather like the neutral chain in Fig. 1. Thus if the polyampholyte term dominates at small distances it continues to dominate at large distances and for all salt concentrations. Even in the case where the polyelectrolyte term dominates [condition (5.5)] it must be remembered that we are discussing "weakly charged." chains in the sense of Khokhlov and Khachaturian. 16 This is the case where the counterion condensation phenomenon (Manning 20 ) can be ignored. The polyelectrolyte blob is then large. It only makes sense to define Kp as in Eq. (5.7) when Ne is large and! - g<f + g. The strongly charged case for polyelectrolyte has been considered by Odijk and Houwaart2 1 and Fixman and Skolnick,22 who show that the polyelectrolyte excluded volume must be treated much more carefully in this case. In general the physical properties of polyelectrolytes are affected by the counterions; a peculiarity of neutral polyampholytes is that it is possible to prepare solutions containing no counterions. Since there is no net charge any counterions can essentially be "washed away". For a single chain this should not lead to important differences but this could play an important role in a more concentrated solution.

Below this temperature the chains tend to attract each other also. We expect a phase separation between a high concentration phase of interpenetrating polymer chains and a dilute solution in which each chain is a collapsed globule. Let PA be the polymer concentration in dilute solution, and PB be the concentration in the dense phase. In a () solvent the optimum concentration in the dense phase is governed by balancing the electrostatic attractions with the third virial term, just as for a single globule. We therefore expectpB ~jllb 4 as within the single collapsed chain. The chain in the dense phase is surrounded by other chain.s, and therefore adopts a Gaussian configuration, as for a chain in a semidilute solution at scales longer than the correlation length. VII. CONCLUSIONS AND COMPARISON WITH EXPERIMENTS We have shown that a neutral or nearly neutral polyampholyte chain collapses due to attractive electrostatic interactions. The charges in the collapsed chain behave like a small volume of Debye-Hiickel electrolyte. In absence of salt the chain may be viewed as a globule of close-packed blobs, each of radius Kp- I, which is the screening length coming from the polymeric charge. In a () solvent the chain is ideal at scales smaller than one blob, and in a good solvent it is swollen. Addition of salt affects the chain configuration if the salt concentration is higher than the concentration of polymeric charge within the globule. When the Coulomb interactions are screened by salt they give rise to a contribution to the excluded volume. For a chain in a good solvent there is a () salt concentration at which the net excluded volume is zero. the chain is swollen above this salt concentration and collapsed below. In all cases a phase separation within the solution is likely at temperatures (or salt concentrations) just below the collapse point of the single polymer. For nonneutral chains we have obtained the range of the

J. Chem. Phys., Vol. 94, No.2, 15 January 1991

VI. PHASE SEPARATION IN SOLUTION

[Page 11]

neutral. They are soluble in water, and the solution viscosity varies with salt concentration in a way that is strongly dependent on the type of ion present. McCormick and Johnson25 have studied a series of polyampholyte copolymers containing varying percentages of positively and negatively charged monomers. These chains are all soluble in water. The solution viscosity in water plotted against the percentage of positive monomers passes through a sharp minimum close to the neutral point. In salt solution the viscosity again passes through a minimum, but the effect is less pronounced and the minimum is spread over a wider range of positive-negative charge difference. This is in agreement with Eq. (6.10) which says that the polyampholyte effect dominates the polyeletrolyte effect over a wider range off - g for higher concentrations of salt. Quantitative interpretation of these results is not possible because of the considerable difference in molecular weight between the chains of different charge ratios. Similar results have been found by Salamone et aF6 who note the preparation of polyampholyte copolymers without the presence of counterions. They have also studied27 polymers in which each monomer contains both a positively and a negatively charged chemical group, known as polyzwitterions. These polymers are again insoluble in water, but become soluble on the addition of salt. The viscosity then increases with salt concentration. In a current investigation Corpart and Candau28 study a series of polyampholytes with different ratios of positive to negative charge. The neutral 50:50 chains are insoluble in water and become soluble at large salt concentrations (- 1 M NaCl). Nonneutral chains with a charge ratio 60:40 or greater are soluble in water. The resulting solution is optically transparent and has a high viscosity, suggesting that the chains are in a highly extended polyelectrolyte configuration. On addition of salt ( <0.1 M NaCl) the 60:40 chain precipitates. We interpret this as the phase separation which occurs when v* becomes negativeatKo>Ke [Eq. (5.9)]. On further addition of salt (~0.8 M NaCl) the chains dissolve. The solution is again optically transparent, and has a lower viscosity than in pure water. This suggests that the chains are in a more or less ideal configuration because all the electrostatic interactions are screened. The 80:20 chains are soluble for all salt concentrations. The solution is transparent for low and high concentrations, but the optical transmission passes through a minimum at intermediate salt concentrations.This again suggests that v* passes through a minimum as predicted by theory, and that there may be partial aggregation of chains. An important feature noted experimentally is the sensitivity of the chain behavior to the particular type ofinorganic ions present. For instance in Ref. 28 the concentration of Nael required to dissolve the polyampholytes differs from that of NaBr and ofNaI. Considerable differences have also been noted in Ref. 24 between HCl, NaC!, and esC!. This suggests that the ionic site is important for a quantitative interpretation. To simplify the theory we have ignored the ion radius in the Debye-Hiickel theory from the beginning. The way in which the ions are solvated in water, and the possibility of specific ion-polymer interactions should also
J. Chem. Phys., Vol. 94, No.2, 15 January 1991

positive-negative charge difference within which the poIyampholyte effect dominates the repulsive polyelectrolyte effect. This range is wider in the presence of salt than in pure water. In some cases nonneutral chains are in an extended polyelectrolyte configuration in pure water, but collapse due to the polyampholyte effect at moderate salt concentrations. At very large concentrations of salt all the electrostatic interactions become negligible and the chain behaves like an uncharged chain. All these results have been obtained within a few approximations that we now briefly discuss. We have assumed that polyampholyte chains are at thermodynamic equilibrium and are not frozen in a metastable state where pairs of charges of opposite sign would stick irreversibly. This seems a reasonable assumption when the interaction between charges is of order kT but the other limit can certainly be reached by lowering temperature or in a solvent of lower dielectric constant. We also have systematically preaveraged the random distribution of charges and assumed there is no correlation between the charges on different monomers. In order to test this approximation we consider, in Appendix B, chains with short range correlations along the chemical sequence, and chains with strictly alternating positive and negative charges. In neither of these cases do the correlations of the charges make a qualitative difference. Finally our theory has been rather rough being limited to scaling and blob arguments. We have discussed the transition between a collapsed and a Gaussian or swollen state for the chains as a function of various parameters but we have not discussed the precise nature ofthe transition and its connection to the usual tricritical obehavior. This merits further investigation, especially for the large excluded volume case discussed in Sec. IV. The basic predictions of the theory presented above are in agreement with many experimental results. Tanford 1 discusses data on polyampholytes which are copolymers of a weak acidic monomer and a weak basic monomer. A similar study has been made by Katchalsky and Miller. 23 Adjusting the pH of the solution changes the number of acid and base groups on the chain which ionize, and therefore changes the net charge on the chain. A plot of reduced viscosity of the solution against pH goes through a clear minimum at the isoelectric point in Ref. 1. In Ref. 23 the polymers are insoluble at this point, but rapidly become soluble for a small shift ofpH to either side. This is in agreement with the basic point that nearly neutral chains tend to collapse and have a small hydrodynamic radius, and consequently a rather low viscosity if soluble. The likelihood of a phase separation associated with the collapse explains the insolubility of the chains at the neutral point in Ref. 23 and most of the references below. Peiffer and Lundberg 24 have made a series of highly charged polyampholytes, containing only charged monomers, and weakly charged polyampholytes, containing neutral spacer monomers between the charges. The highly charged chains are insoluble in water, but become soluble on addition oflarge amounts of salt. The solution viscosity continues to increase with increasing salt concentration, although sometimes the trend is reversed at very high salt concentrations, probably due to polymer ion interactions specific to the ion type. The weakly charged chains are non-

[Page 12]

be considered for a more complete theory. We remind the reader that the original Debye-Hiickel theory is only valid at low concentrations Kol < 1, and therefore our theory will not be quantitatively accurate for the high salt concentrations needed to dissolve the highly charged polyampholytes. Finally we note that a computer simulation of neutral polyampholytes is in progress (Victoe9 ). The collapse transition is observed in both two and three dimensions.

Up to now we have assumed that charges on different monomers are independent, and hence for n i= m, (cnc m ) = if - g)2. Combined with the preaveraging assumption, this means that the first order electrostatic term in B * is exactly zero for neutral chains. Here we consider neutral chains where the monomers are not independent. The first order term is then no longer exactly zero, and we ask under what circumstances it becomes important with re-

We see that zp does not depend on N, and therefore is always small for small p. In fact this effect alone would lead to a very small renormalization of the monomer length, but the chain would remain Gaussian. The form of zp is the same as Z. for a pure polyelectrolyte, except that N in Ze has been replaced by p. The correlations will therefore begin to become important if p - N e • At this point each correlated region is sufficiently long to behave like a polyelectrolyte itself. It is unlikely that slight differences in the reaction rates could lead to correlations anywhere near this range. We conclude therefore that correlations of the type (BI) do not affect the chain behavior. However it is possible to deliberately form charged block copolymers where this effect will be important. Such chains will not be well described by the random polyampholyte theory. Varoqui et al. 30 have studied diblock copolymers with the two halves of opposite sign. The viscosity of these solu-

During the polymerization procedure there may be different rates of reaction for addition of the different types of monomers to the chain which will depend on the monomers already present at the end of the chain. This leads to short range correlations between neighboring monomers. McCormick and Johnson25 have investigated this type of correlation in their polyampholyte chains. We suppose here that charged monomers within a distance p monomers along the chain have a tendency to be of the same sign.
